unit.name: Video datasets, Sports-1M results, and C3D
unit.description: Discusses Sports-1M, empirical comparisons of simple and 3D models, dataset distribution issues, and the C3D architecture.
unit.summary: After covering architectures, the lecture asks what data can train video classifiers, using Sports-1M as a major early dataset for fine-grained sports classification with 487 categories [ts=1921s] [ts=1939s] [ts=1972s]. Results on Sports-1M show a surprising lesson: the single-frame baseline already performs strongly, early fusion can be slightly worse, late fusion is slightly better, and a 2014-style 3D CNN gives only a modest 2–3% boost in that setting [ts=1989s] [ts=2013s] [ts=2031s]. The segment also notes video datasets are harder to distribute than ImageNet because videos are huge; Sports-1M was shared as YouTube URLs, but many videos disappear or change, making the dataset unstable over time [ts=2083s] [ts=2108s] [ts=2124s]. C3D is introduced as an early popular 3D CNN, essentially a VGG-like architecture with 3 x 3 x 3 convolutions and 2 x 2 x 2 pooling, trained on Sports-1M and widely reused as a pretrained feature extractor [ts=2149s] [ts=2179s] [ts=2227s]. Its drawback is compute: converting 2D VGG-style computation to 3D raises cost to about 39.5 GFLOPs, roughly 2.9 times VGG-16, motivating later improvements such as residual connections and better architectures [ts=2296s] [ts=2334s] [ts=2376s].

key_points.text: Sports-1M is an early large video dataset with very fine-grained sports categories, including 487 sports classes.
On Sports-1M, the single-frame baseline reaches strong top-five accuracy, early fusion is slightly worse, late fusion slightly better, and 3D CNNs add about 2–3%.
Video datasets distributed as YouTube URL lists can become unstable because videos are modified or deleted.
C3D is VGG-like but replaces 2D image operations with 3D convolution and pooling, such as 3 x 3 x 3 conv and 2 x 2 x 2 pooling.
Pretrained C3D became popular as a feature extractor for researchers who could not afford to train large video models.
C3D is computationally expensive: about 39.5 GFLOPs versus 13.6 GFLOPs for VGG-16 and 0.7 GFLOPs for AlexNet.

kp_catalog.name: Sports-1M and video dataset instability
kp_catalog.description: Sports-1M is a fine-grained sports video dataset originally distributed as YouTube URLs, making it vulnerable to deleted or modified videos.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because sports-1M and video dataset instability clarifies sports-1M is a fine-grained sports video dataset originally distributed as YouTube URLs, making it vulnerable to deleted or modified videos, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: C3D
kp_catalog.description: C3D is an early VGG-like 3D CNN trained on Sports-1M and widely used as a pretrained feature extractor, but it is compute-heavy.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because c3D clarifies c3D is an early VGG-like 3D CNN trained on Sports-1M and widely used as a pretrained feature extractor, but it is compute-heavy, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Single-frame CNN baseline
kp_catalog.description: A simple video classifier can sample frames, run a 2D CNN independently, and average probabilities.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because single-frame CNN baseline clarifies a simple video classifier can sample frames, run a 2D CNN independently, and average probabilities, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
